query: are toll-like receptors induced by microbes

retrieved_docs.title: Phagocytosis -- Issues of Concern -- Receptor-lead Activation
retrieved_docs.content: In the case of opsonin-dependent receptors (such as Fc-gamma and complement receptors), secondary signals must become activated to complete the initiation of phagocytosis. Specifically, the Nf-kB complex (Nf-kBc) must activate to initiate specific gene transcription. Nf-kBc activation becomes stimulated by pattern recognition receptors (PRRs). The induction of PRRs is by receptor ligands known as pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs). Phagocytic cells also contain other receptors, such as scavenger receptors and LPS receptors, that stimulate phagocytosis without the use of an opsonin. Such receptors bind to ligands such as PAMPs and damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) to stimulate phagocytosis. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Gram-Negative Bacteria -- Introduction -- Antibiotic Resistance
retrieved_docs.content: GNB have two membranes, an external and an internal. The external membrane expresses a potent immune response inducer, lipopolysaccharide (LPS), which is composed of three units: a hydrophilic polysaccharide, O antigen, and a hydrophobic domain known as lipid A. Lipid A are responsible for the higher endotoxic activity of these bacteria. [13] [14] However, the LPS is heterogeneous in the various bacterial groups, and some bacteria manifest this antigen weakly due to genetic changes and are not recognized by Toll-like receptors. In contrast, there are BGN groups that can trigger such a response in large proportions. Thus, LPS can trigger the innate immune response through Toll-like receptors 4 (TLR4), which occurs in many immune cells such as monocytes, macrophages, dendritic cells, and neutrophils. The resulting activation of the innate immune response mediated by LPS together with TLR4 receptors culminates in an exacerbated response with the production of cytokines, chemokines, and interferons and their suppression.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Inflammatory Response -- Biochemical and Genetic Pathology
retrieved_docs.content: Numerous mediators play a crucial role in initiating the cascade of the acute inflammatory process. The first group of mediators comprises toll-like receptors (TLRs), membrane-spanning proteins found on the surfaces of innate immune system cells such as macrophages and dendritic cells. These single-pass membrane-spanning receptors recognize the pathogen-associated molecular patterns or can recognize endogenous signals activated during tissue or cell damage known as danger-associated molecular patterns. [9] To date, research has identified more than 10 TLRs. An important example is the CD14 (cluster of differentiation 14), a co-receptor for TLR4, which is on the surface of innate immune system cells preferentially expressed in macrophages, monocytes, and neutrophils. TLR4 recognizes lipopolysaccharide, the major component of the outer membrane of gram-negative bacteria (pathogen-associated molecular patterns). Then, the transmission of pathogen-associated molecular patterns and danger-associated molecular patterns are mediated by the adapter protein MyD88 (myeloid differentiation 88) and the TLRs. Subsequently, signaling is transmitted through a specific cascade, leading to the nuclear translocation of transcription factors such as nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells (NF-kB), activator protein-1 (AP-1), or interferon regulatory factor 3 (IRF3). [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipopolysaccharide -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Interestingly, canonical Lipid A, such as from non-invading E coli, is speculated to be agonistic, while less conical lipid A, such as P. gingivalis, may activate a different signal (such as TLR4 instead of TLR2 and the strictly cylindrical lipid A, as Rhodobacter sphaeroides , tend to be antagonistic to TLRs. Hence, more pathogenic bacteria have evolved their lipid A moiety to evade host immune attacks.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipopolysaccharide -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Of the three components, lipid A is the most bioactive component of LPS and a potent part of the endotoxin response generated by a molecule. Thus, LPS can be used for the early detection of infection since it induces an innate immune response, specifically through Toll-like receptors (TLRs). Even when the immune system causes lysis of the bacterial cells, lipid A containing fragments of the membrane released in the circulation cause fever, diarrhea, and, in adverse circumstances, lead to septic (endotoxic) shock. Though lipid A moiety is a very conserved part of the LPS, the structure differs among different strains, species, and subspecies of bacteria. [5] Hence, the overall immune activation and response depend upon the structure of lipid A moiety of LPS. LPS recognition by the host is crucial for clearing the infections of invading bacterial pathogens. On the other hand, most gram-negative bacteria show innate resistance to many antimicrobial therapies due to the presence of LPS because it develops a permeability barrier at the cell surface.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Acute Phase Reactants -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Macrophages sense bacterial products through Toll-like receptors (TLR) and regulate the inflammatory response. TLR4 senses the gram-negative bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS). Downstream signaling leads to the activation of the transcription factor, nuclear factor kappa beta (NF-kB), and increased production of IL-6. IL-6 activates liver production of other acute-phase proteins. Tissue injury and trauma can also activate the acute-phase reaction (APR). The purpose of acute-phase proteins released during APR is to participate in blood coagulation, defense against infection, transport metabolites, nutrients and hormones, and maintenance of homeostasis. IL-6, IL-1beta, TNF-alpha, glucocorticoids, and growth factors are the primary mediators of acute-phase proteins (APP) gene expression. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipopolysaccharide -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Enhance the effect of Vaccines and Drugs: TLR activation is inducible by low levels of endotoxin, present in vaccines and other immunotherapeutics, which provides improved immunization and drug effect, leading to better therapeutic outcomes. Low levels of LPS present in adjuvant can enhance their activity. Hence, the term for it is innate immune response modulation impurities (IIRMIs). [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Sepsis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In healthy hosts, infection causes an inflammatory response that is ideally localized and controlled. This host response is protective against microbial proliferation and dissemination. Certain microorganisms contain molecular constituents, such as gram-negative bacterial lipopolysaccharide and fungal beta-D-glucan, termed pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs). These PAMPs are recognized by pattern recognition receptors (PRRs), including but not limited to Toll-like receptors (TLRs), which initiate an inflammatory response. Additionally, the release of intracellular contents into the circulation by damaged host cells, termed damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), potentiates the host inflammatory response. These molecules activate the TLRs of antigen-presenting cells (APCs) and monocytes, increasing the expression of various transcription factors, including nuclear factor-κB and AP-1. This subsequently results in the release of proinflammatory interleukins (IL), tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), cytokines, interferons (IFNs), and chemokines. [12] [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Herpes Simplex Keratitis -- Pathophysiology -- Toll-like receptors
retrieved_docs.content: Toll-like receptors (TLRs) represent a fundamental aspect of the innate immune system, characterized by their ubiquitous presence and capacity to detect specific pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs). To this end, 13 TLRs have been identified, with 9 common to humans and mice. [44] Each TLR is tailored to recognize distinct PAMPs or ligands. In the context of HSV-1, TLRs 2, 3, and 9 play pivotal roles in identifying viral components; TLR2 interacts with viral glycoproteins at the cellular membrane, whereas TLR3 and TLR9, located within endosomes, are responsive to double-stranded RNA and unmethylated CpG motifs, respectively. The engagement of these TLRs with their respective PAMPs initiates a series of intracellular signaling pathways that culminate in cytokine production, inflammation, and the activation of an innate immune defense. [45] While the activation of TLRs constitutes a critical early mechanism for viral detection and initiation of immune responses, excessive TLR-mediated inflammation can lead to detrimental outcomes, including increased infiltration of the cornea, resulting in opacity and potential vision loss. These outcomes highlight the importance of a balanced immune response, where sufficient viral clearance is achieved without provoking adverse inflammatory reactions that compromise ocular health. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipopolysaccharide -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, LPS is the primary component that imparts pathogenicity to the bacterial cell. Compared to the classical bacterial exotoxins, the endotoxins (LPS) are quite stable and are the primary biological response modifiers to induce specific symptoms and pathologies of the diseases. LPS exerts a potent and pleiotropic stimulus of host immune cells. The immune system recognizes the Lipid A component of LPS, which is released from the dividing cells in soluble form, from the lysed bacterial cells upon autolysis, or killing by complement activation or phagocytosis or by the effect of antibiotics. The primary response is through the identification and binding of the lipid A component to the TLR4 of the host cell. Since Lipid A is quite variable in its composition, different strains of bacteria mount different levels of the immune response. Lipid A produced by E. coli and Salmonella is highly immunogenic, while others, such as that of Yersinia pestis , modulate the extent of acylation of lipid A upon infection to produce LPS of low immunogenicity in vivo. Interestingly, synthesizing an LPS of low immunogenicity is a bacterial strategy to evade host immune response and increase intracellular survival. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome -- Etiology -- Pathogen-Associated Molecular Pattern-Related (Infectious) Etiologies
retrieved_docs.content: PAMPs are microbial-derived molecules that trigger immune responses via pathogen-recognition receptors, including: Bacterial infections (eg, pneumonia, pyelonephritis, and cellulitis) Viral infections (eg, influenza and SARS-CoV-2) Disseminated fungal infections, particularly in immunocompromised hosts Toxic shock syndrome (due to staphylococcal or streptococcal exotoxins and endotoxins) PAMP-induced SIRS can range from localized infections to disseminated infections and bacteremia, ultimately progressing to sepsis or septic shock if organ dysfunction ensues.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Receptors -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Although cellular receptors primarily bind to endogenous ligands, 2 notable exceptions exist—pathogenic viruses, which can bind to host cellular receptors to infect a cell, and bacterial components, which can bind to receptors on immune cells to initiate an immune response. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Fever in a Neonate -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The activation of the pattern recognition receptors (PRR) like extracellular, toll-like receptors (TLRs), and intracellular NOD-like receptors (NLR) and RIG-like receptors (RLR) helps in recognition of a pathogen by the local immunity.  Gram-positive, gram-negative sepsis activates TLR2 and TLR4, respectively; whereas, the double-stranded virus activates TLR3. They start an immune response by producing pro-inflammatory cytokines which cause endothelial activation and damage-causing SIRS, sepsis, and death. Premature neonates show dysregulation complement system making them susceptible to severe sepsis. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Keratoconjunctivitis -- Pathophysiology -- Inflammatory Cascade and Immune Response
retrieved_docs.content: In bacterial keratoconjunctivitis, bacterial components such as lipopolysaccharides (LPS) activate toll-like receptors (TLRs) on corneal and conjunctival epithelial cells. Downstream signaling pathways are then triggered, producing chemokines that recruit polymorphonuclear leukocytes (PMNs) to the infection site. Polymorphonuclear leukocytes contribute to tissue inflammation, ulceration, and discharge. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipopolysaccharide -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Inside the host system, the lipid A moiety of LPS is detected at picomolar levels by the receptor on the surface of macrophages and endothelial cells. Initially, LPS binds to the LBP in the serum, which transfers it to the CD14 receptor present on the cell membrane of the immune cells. The CD14 transfers it to the MD2 (a non-anchored protein), which interacts with Toll-like receptor-4 (TLR4). Thus, LPS binds with the CD14/TLR4/MD2 receptor complex present in many host cell types, such as monocytes, dendritic cells, macrophages, and B cells. The subsequent response depends on the cell type to which LPS is bound. [17] In monocytes and macrophages, three plausible responses, mentioned below, are triggered;

retrieved_docs.title: Rosacea -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Activation of the adaptive and innate immune system response by overexpression of Th1/Th17 and toll-like receptor 2 (TLR-2), respectively, are other known pathomechanisms for rosacea. TLR-2 activation results in increased activity of mast cells via an increase in LL-37 production. In addition, expression of matrix metalloproteinases and vascular endothelial growth factor is increased in rosacea. [2] [4] In rosacea, microbes may trigger activation of the immune response. This hypothesis is supported by an increased number of organisms, such as Demodex folliculorum on the skin and helicobacter pylori infection in the gut of patients with rosacea. [2] [6] [8]

retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylmuramyl-alanyl-isoglutamin is classified as an immunostimulant, meaning it belongs to the group of agents that activate or enhance the body’s immune response. As an immunostimulant, acetylmuramyl-alanyl-isoglutamin can be used to boost immune function in various clinical contexts, supporting the body’s ability to recognize and combat pathogens or abnormal cells.

Toxoids, which are inactivated toxins, are used to safely stimulate protective immune physiology without causing disease. Together, these diverse organisms, molecules, anatomical structures, and pharmacologic agents illustrate the wide range of factors that influence human physiological processes and underscore their importance in clinical assessment, diagnosis, and therapeutic intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1636
retrieved_docs.content: Lactobacillus is classified as a microbial agent‑containing product, meaning it is a microorganism incorporated into formulations that deliver live bacterial agents for therapeutic or health‑promoting purposes. As a probiotic bacterium, Lactobacillus is used in dietary supplements, fermented foods, and medical preparations to support gut microbiota balance, enhance immune function, and aid in the prevention or treatment of certain infections. Recognizing Lactobacillus as a microbial agent‑containing product highlights its role in patient care as a biologically active component of probiotic therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2706
retrieved_docs.content: Inosin pranobex is classified as an immunological adjuvant, indicating that it functions to enhance the body’s immune response. This categorization reflects its role in modulating immunity and supporting the body’s defense mechanisms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1279
retrieved_docs.content: suppresses T‑cell‑driven biosynthetic responses; racemic adrenaline stimulates metabolic pathways that feed biosynthetic cycles; and trisaccharides serve as carbohydrate precursors incorporated into complex biosynthetic reactions. Inhibitory interleukins further regulate biosynthesis by modulating immune‑cell function. Collectively, this integrated network underscores that immunosuppressants are essential tools for controlling interleukin‑mediated immune activation, are often derived from or characterized by specialized biosynthetic mechanisms, and interact with multiple metabolic and biosynthetic pathways—knowledge that assists clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies and understanding the biochemical basis of these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2831
retrieved_docs.content: Inosin pranobex, also known by the abbreviation ISA, is classified as an immunostimulant; it belongs to the broader group of immunostimulants that are used to enhance the body’s immune response in various clinical settings. This connection highlights the role of inosin pranobex/ISA as a therapeutic agent that modulates immunity, which is relevant for the management of viral infections and other conditions where immune activation is desired.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1759
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified as a therapeutic intervention and is a subcategory of the broader group termed Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This relationship indicates that preventive vaccination against bacterial pathogens is considered part of the overall therapeutic strategies that include various forms of immunization, injectable treatments, and perfusion techniques, highlighting its role within comprehensive patient care aimed at preventing infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1949
retrieved_docs.content: Tolpa and acetylmuramyl‑alanyl‑isoglutamin are both classified as immunostimulants, agents that enhance or modulate the activity of the immune system. By acting as immunostimulants, these substances can promote immune cell activation, increase cytokine production, and improve the body’s ability to respond to infections or malignancies, making them relevant in therapeutic strategies aimed at strengthening host defenses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2642
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is identified as a specific subset within the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, indicating that it falls under the general group of preventive immunizations. The category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions as a classification that encompasses various preventive vaccines and inoculations, including those targeted at bacterial pathogens. This hierarchical relationship clarifies that bacterial disease‑specific prophylactic vaccines are organized under the overarching concept of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, which serves to group together all preventive immunization strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2100
retrieved_docs.content: Injection or infusion of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases are both classified under the broader category of Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This parent category encompasses medical procedures that deliver therapeutic agents directly into the body, either through injection or infusion, as well as preventive measures such as bacterial vaccinations. By grouping these specific interventions within the same therapeutic framework, clinicians recognize that both the administration of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and the use of bacterial vaccines are integral components of immunization and perfusion strategies aimed at preventing disease and delivering treatment effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 501
retrieved_docs.content: Meningitis, an inflammation of the protective membranes covering the brain and spinal cord, encompasses several specific forms, including bacterial meningitis (التهاب السحايا الجرثومي), which represents a distinct subtype caused by bacterial infection. Recognizing bacterial meningitis as a particular category within the broader condition of meningitis is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted antimicrobial therapy, and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2568

This specific preventive measure is placed under the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, indicating that it has a parent relationship to that general class. The overarching class of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions to classify and organize these preventive immunization strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 669
retrieved_docs.content: The community recognizes that a diverse set of microorganisms, bioactive compounds, hormones, receptors, and resistance mechanisms—such as Aspergillus fumigatus, Capsicum, Vibrio cholerae, alantoin, viral antigens, cyclooxygenase‑2, diiodotyrosine, dinoprost, enfuvirtide, exenatide, the peptide enkephalin Ala(2)‑MePhe(4)‑Gly(5), cannabinoid receptors, streptanthins, helminths, human insulin, kalidin, volatile fatty acids, the amino acid proline, trimethoprim‑resistance mechanisms, and toxoids—interact within human physiological systems to modulate immune responses, metabolic pathways, and disease processes. These entities illustrate the extensive network of factors that underlie normal function, pathogenesis, and therapeutic intervention. In addition, xenon is identified as an “allowed qualifier” within physiological contexts, indicating its relevance to physiological studies.
